item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It may not seem like a long time
but it is an eternity
to a fly

Honestly this line made me give it a +1 after I had left it at a no-vote for a while

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: should the opportunity arise vomit me flies

item.title: Re: SCipherP-3063
item.content:encoded: it's a deal with the devil they're fairly common.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Devil's advocate rolled into a telepathic fly.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beelzebub
Lord of the flies

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, but I'm curious about whether it appears to foundation personnel as a preference, or if it just appears to lots of people a lot of times. If so, I'm curious about when it started doing this, and if it's more than like 100 years ago, why there's no folklore or stories about it. Still, it would be hard to answer those in an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, as if SCP-738 wasn't enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Couldn't see what's so specific about 2376 days until I converted that to years, months, and days. Nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the containment procedure is kill it.
But killing it makes it pop up somewhere else, still targeting the same person, so you have achieved nothing except giving the person a very short reprieve from temptation.
And it does this less than once a year.
And something like 10% of the deals ever made are with foundation staff.

What am I missing, because that doesn't make sense.

Edit: Shouldn't the main list title be capitalized? Honestly unsure if that is on purpose or is a rule but I don't think lower case adds anything here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What else can they do during the 2000 days? I don't see an alternative to a fly that doesn't manifest until the last deal's debt has been paid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can check previous versions of pages through the "History" link on the bottom of the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies for the necropost; What were the contents of the hidden collapsible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea isn't really interesting imo. Just another OP keter that can't be destroyed, which is something I personally don't prefer.
And also, number in this skip kinda lack of consideration. 2300+ days are near 7 years. It is very unconvincing that fondation took this many years to do the experiment and just wait to see if the results are different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only question I have is why are Doctors and professors are making these deals? Couldn't they just get a D-Class and say "Ask for this, then your done"? It would require lesser losses and such.

From the article…

The terms of such agreement vary greatly, but almost always involve SCP-3063 promising an individual any single thing they greatly desire in exchange for an unspecified price.

And consider that most D-class are often seen as prison inmates on death row (or are otherwise really nasty people).

Do you want to take the risk of letting people who shouldn't be out on the streets negotiating with the dealmaking fly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only question I have is why are Doctors and professors are making these deals? Couldn't they just get a D-Class and say "Ask for this, then your done"? It would require lesser losses and such.

Besides that I enjoy the SCP, though I feel as though the requests are down to a smaller scale (Why wouldn't someone ask for the death of 682 or the permanent containment/death of 2317? It just seems a bit over done in where someone could get anything they wanted)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is incredible, I was upvoting anyway but the hidden text really goes the extra mile, making it deeply personal and existentially terrifying in a way it already was- but not to quite such an extent.

Lovely lovely lovely. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of me wishes you didn't tell us about the hidden text, just so that someone could accidentally find it at some point and have a "Holy shit moment. The other part of me is glad you did tell me cuz I wouldn't have found it otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, hey, it's another one of these things. Another severed head screaming at us in French that we don't engineer around because the author says so. Another invincible lizard that can totally die because it's the adaptive nature of life except for when it's not life because being life would get it killed.

I would say "The current manifestation of SCP-3063 to live for at least 100 million years as humans reckon time, and for neither it nor any of its children to ever leave this room." Then I'd go get my goddamned lunch.

Neutralization is for chumps. Contain.

item.title: Caught some mistakes.
item.content:encoded: No manifestation event has been observed as of yet, and it is not currently know (needs to be corrected to 'known'.) whether SCP-3063 manifest (needs an s at the end.) as an entirely new individual housefly, or simply inhabits the body of an existing housefly.

item.title: SCipherP-3063
item.content:encoded: I swear that you took inspiration from Gravity Falls. Anyone see Bill Cipher here? Cuz I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good use of hidden text and body horror, but it feels very by-the-numbers and uninspired.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The hidden text makes this article
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see zero resemblance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But still. The use of phrases like "immediately," "ultimate," "under no circumstances," and "every effort" makes it seem way more important than it actually is. The consequences are not dire enough to warrant using those phrases.

Also, now that I think more about it, my first point is a serious problem for this. You're telling me that I came up with that two minutes after reading this, but a huge Foundation full of super geniuses didn't think of it throughout their entire existence? The main counterargument would be that flies only live for a month and this is a physiologically normal fly, so once it dies it escapes containment. However, the fact that it always returns to the same individual means that if it chooses a Foundation employee, it will always just go straight back to them. Once again, this is still temporary since it only lasts until the chosen individual dies, but it's a hell of a lot more effective than the current containment procedures (i.e. nothing).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if this SCP is related to SCP-409

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why is the Foundation so hell-bent on containing and/or prematurely killing people who have made deals?

Because it is not normal for people to have flies growing inside of them. And it can be a mercy kill to a very horrible existence with a host to insects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have two issues with this.

1. Nowhere does it mention that this fly is capable of teleportation. What is stopping them from just trapping the fly in a cup with a piece of paper underneath it? I would assume that reality benders can teleport, but you need to make this explicit.

2. If the flies that hatch after the person's death are non-anomalous, why is the Foundation so hell-bent on containing and/or prematurely killing people who have made deals? You make it seem like failing to do so will cause some super terrible thing to happen, but all that happens is that a bunch of non-anomalous bugs appear. The Foundation should not be wasting their time with that. If there is a better reason to have containment be such a big deal, you need to specify that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked where this was going, but was upset that it didn't seem to actually get there. I felt like there should have been some degree of rhyme or reason to this formicophobia bait; like some revelation that this was all part of some Kyuubei-tier deal that you would volunteer to help maintain Earth's population of ecologically(or perhaps even metaphysically)-essential insects in exchange for one wish, and basically you _would_ accept eventually or else the world dies horribly, but alas, all those flies just die after eating you and it turns out Meflystophiles is just an asshole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For those of you who said you'd upvote if this wasn't a contest entry: it's not anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this, but I do feel like there's something missing to really bring the horror element out and make this stand out.
Furthermore, I don't feel like this is exactly 3000 worth. No vote for this, but if you bring it back for a normal article I would definetly +1 it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately, this feels a bit more contrived than horrifying.

The fly thing is pretty gross, sure. I had a moment of shuddering trying to imagine it, which is good. But the horror element is weak here; you go only slightly further than most horror variations of this story would, and it's undercut by them being able to just kill themselves to end their suffering. (But if you make them unable to do so, then that feels contrived too.)

The Foundation doctors in the test logs are too un-clever, which makes it worse that they're senior researchers. I simply don't believe they wouldn't have better attempts. The fifth test is particularly bad because it so obviously was going to just be the next test log, or some similarly completely pointless tiny piece of new information.

I'm unconvinced that the Foundation wouldn't just contain the person currently being harassed and have them never accept the deal ever. Not great for that person, but who cares?

It's worse than them just not being clever, though. I just can't comprehend why the Foundation doesn't immediately get that this entity will just cheat to ensure it can't be contained. If it could be contained, why on earth would it allow itself to be put in a position where it would? And this is without being aware that it can see the future. It's a reality-bending all-powerful genie/devil that can select its targets, meaning it can pick anyone in the world that it likes, ever. It's never going to put itself at risk and anyone with sufficient smarts would be able to figure that out.

And another question, why has no one wished for no SCP-3000-related harm to come to them, or some other variation? Sure that may not be their intrinsic desire, but any Foundation scientist would know they aren't gonna get what they really want — and they don't try anyway, they just try (in a really half-assed way) to get it to contain itself. It probably wouldn't work, but it seems more likely than the other attempts.

Honestly, this feels like a story I've seen a lot before, and not done in any new or interesting way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, Beezlebub.

I don't really understand why it keeps harassing the Foundation specifically, or why the Foundation wastes the opportunity to contain it when it does. All they need to do is just ignore it indefinitely no matter how ridiculous the offer is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, so I will 1+ it.

It is hard to explain why I like it. I just do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Reality-bending trickster djinni fly" is a fucking amazing concept for an article. Unfortunately, this fails to deliver, wasting it on fairly unimpressive body horror and a narrative about its interactions with the Foundation that isn't particularly compelling or sensible. I wish I didn't have to downvote this because I love the beginning part, but from it falls flat once the full details of its effects start being revealed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this. it made me very uncomfortable. ew

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Devoted for now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why hasn't anyone ever said, "contain this and all future instances?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I donno, the fact that this is linked to the devil might be scary to some.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but:

1) I don't think it's 3000 material. It's gross rather than scary, and 3000 needs to be something extra-spacial.
2) The test logs could showcase the Foundation and the item being a lot more clever/cold than they do.

Test 1: Permanent containment
Result: Combustion, eggs grow after 2376 days.
Note: Evidently containment is not control.

Test 2: Complete documentation on item, including history and containment procedure
Result: 10,000 pages written in an unknown language (SCP-XXXX-1), then same as result 1
Note: Proposal to request translation of SCP-XXXX-1 from SCP-XXXX rejected.

Test 3: Non-existence of SCP-XXXX, in all instances, everywhere in the universe from now onward
Result: SCP-XXXX replaced with a new species of fly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of something talking to you, but I don't really understand the white-text fourth-wall breaking and how it pairs with the effect of the fly wishing and dying.

Admittedly, this is a horrible way to die. Which is really cool. I super appreciate that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For what it's worth: I have removed the collapsible. I acknowledge that is was heavyhanded, and more of a detriment than anything else. If that changes anyone's mind, I'd appreciate a reappraisal, if not then I understand. Thanks for reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm starting to not like the collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This would be a perfectly fine article (excellent body horror!) if not for that collapsible. It just killed the whole thing, driving the point home instead of letting it hang unspoken.
The white text was fine, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fly eggs are fucking gross (and hit me right in the phobia) and made me shudder, but the deal concept and the collapsible are pretty 'meh' for me. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had it not been a potential entry for a milestone skip, i would have up-voted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote. I like the taunting white text, but the general idea of an omnipresent Satan fly just doesn't fit with the theme, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, not really feeling this. There is definitely a lot of potential in a wish granting fly, both as an anomaly and a character, but such potential is used up to have the fly play host to simple body horror. The final collapsible, while providing a neat bit of characterization for 3000, really wasn't enough to win me over.

-1. Just wasn't that scary for me as a reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was an upvote until that last collapsible, but I suspect that's a personal thing: I'm kind of like Hobbs in this Calvin and Hobbs strip; I don't really want anything that badly, and what I do want is stuff I can get myself. (Don't even get me started on power = happiness. Not sure what Nietzsche was on when he wrote that nonsense.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a good SCP. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe bugs just really get to me, maybe I have a weak spot for the meta repetition (if anyone says anything about bodies and/or water, I will fear-piss right now); but on a scale of one-to-ten, this is off the charts for me in terms of fear. I had a strong, unnerving reaction to this, doubly so now that I discovered the collapsible. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "hidden" collapsible is not to my liking. I think you can do better than a threat.

I still think that Islip-18 can easily be with the procedures, and that you should have done more showing than telling here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The core tension between good things now and terrible things later is fertile ground for horror, and while the repeated line thing is a little overdone in recent site history, it's a good line you've chosen, and it's deployed well.

But having presumably started from that point, the architecture of the skip doesn't support the premise. There is no particular reason for the hidden text / collapsible to exist (incidentally, the hidden text - once I was off mobile and could read it - undermines the effect of the collapsible). The tests don't give us a strong example of the "battle of wits / ironic comeuppance" narrative of cursed-wish items - the fly effectively cheats. This means the core of the skip is just marking time waiting for the collapsible.

And in turn (because the reader is less engaged in the narrative), that means that distracting details in the core skip are more obvious:

  • while you mention that the entity is telepathic, the reference to a "physiologically non-anomalous" meant that I struggled to imagine how the fly would make an agreement with an individual (charades?)
  • you assume one manifestation per 6.5 years throughout history - that suggests no-one dies prior to their 6.5 years running out. How can the Foundation assume that?
  • more to the point, there will have been fewer than 20 agreements made in the history of the Foundation (and an unknown, but presumably relatively small, number of destructions without agreement). These could have happened anywhere in the world, and the wish could have been for anything - the most common wish is probably for food. How could the Foundation have possibly tracked these agreements?
  • why does SCP-3000 concentrate on the Foundation? 6 agreements out of 20 is an exceptionally high ratio.
  • why do the Containment Procedures concentrate on destroying manifestations? That seems incredibly difficult to achieve (as how will the Foundation know that they're happening?) and counter-productive, as it just leads to more manifestations and chances of breaking the veil. The better option is to allow agreements (ideally for mundane things) - that at least means there are no more manifestations for 6.5 years.
  • I also don't understand the need for Islip-18 or the murder of individuals before their natural death (or starving them in containment) - the flies produced upon death are non-anomalous, so not particularly a concern to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's definitely in line with what I'm suggesting, though, and I do think you can build on it by taking the next step with a lot of stuff you've set up in the main description and containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article only rehashes the core elements of SCP-738, SCP-2682, and SCP-2852, and the scariest thing is how cliched the hidden collapsible is. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really like the way that this takes "Beelzebub as an SCP" and just rolls with it; the deal-with-the-devil-for-body-horror-by-flies feels like it works, and the hidden text felt like it enhanced the effect. I'm hesitating to vote at present because I'm not really feeling the way that the tests (mainly the last 2?) were done.

Thinking about this, but leaning towards upvote.

EDIT: Been revisiting a few times… I feel like I'm in agreement with other comments that something's missing. Maybe a demonstration of its power other than trolling researchers, or containment procedures which make more sense for long-term sustainability, or a better reason for why they can't just lock up an individual fly once they find it (maybe progressively more start generating?)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did ya find the last collapsible? gotcha

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's gross, and it's got a good setup, but it fails to complete its horror. There's no moment where you look into the abyss and it looks back into you. I think there are multiple places where this could be rectified, there's mention in the containment procedures of not letting Foundation personnel interact with this fly devil.

I enjoyed the classic deal with the devil setup and I think there's horror to be mined from a fly that persists until it makes you ask for what you want and then punishes you for being greedy. But as this stands it focuses more on the biological aspect than the psychological.

+ show block
– hide block

e: The hidden text is nice, but also not enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall, this was a weird idea executed competently enough. But I'm just not personally feeling it. Although the hidden text at the end with the build up was done nicely. No vote, currently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I'm not really feeling this one. The effect is interesting, but it wasn't…. scary to me. I dunno. That's what I use to judge 3000 articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahhhh here it is. I sincerely hope reading this made you uncomfortable. I'll write more here in the future, but it is late and I am stressed.

There is hidden text between each paragraph.

Picture is by Gladson Machado, used under CCSA 3.0
